section.title: Introduction and Overview
section.p: The Allocate application stores an allocation request as a binary data file with RISC OS filetype &FB0. The file contains the requestor's developer details followed by a sequence of allocation request objects. This chapter documents that file format, the related developer details file used by the application, and the text exports generated from the same data.

The binary request file is intended to preserve a request while it is being edited or transferred between Allocate windows. The application can also generate a CSV export containing only reservation requests, a plain text reply-form template, and a text file suitable for email which combines a human-readable summary with a uuencoded copy of the binary request.

subsection.title: Conventions
subsection.p: All integer fields are written as native C int values by the historical implementation. In the supplied sources these are 32-bit little-endian values. Text fields are fixed-length byte arrays written exactly as stored in memory.

Fixed-length text fields are not length-prefixed. The application initialises new structures to zero, then copies user text into them, so unused bytes are normally NUL padded. Readers should treat the last byte of each field as part of the stored field width rather than as guaranteed spare space.

The request file magic is the eight ASCII bytes ALLOCATE with no terminator. Earlier notes in doc/FileFormat describe this as an 8-character string; the implementation confirms that exactly eight bytes are stored.

subsection.title: Binary Request File
subsection.p: Allocate request files have RISC OS filetype &FB0. They are written by the application's Save request operation and read when a request file is opened or dragged into an Allocate window.

offset-table.offset: ASCII ALLOCATE, without a terminator.
File format version multiplied by 100. The supplied implementation writes 100.
Number of request objects following the developer details block.
Developer details block; see the next subsection.
Sequence of entries.

subsubsection.title: Developer Details Block
subsubsection.p: The developer details block is stored inline in every binary request file. When a file is loaded, these details may optionally replace the current session details. The same structure is also used for the standalone details file described later in this chapter.

offset-table.offset: Requestor name.
Company or organisation name.
Developer number. The bundled help states that this field may be left blank.
First address line.
Second address line.
Third address line.
Fourth address line.
Telephone number.
Fax number.
Email address.

subsubsection.title: Object Records
subsubsection.p: Each request object begins with a two-word header:

offset-table.offset: Object type identifier.
Size in bytes of the object payload that follows, not including this header.
Object-specific data for the type named by id.

category.title: SWI Chunk Object
category.p: Requests a SWI chunk and its name prefix.

offset-table.offset: Requested SWI prefix.
Description of the module or use.

category.title: Message Block Object
category.p: Requests a Wimp message block. The user interface exposes a mandatory block required tick, but the saved object stores only the description text.

offset-table.offset: Description of the protocol or message usage.

category.title: Reservation Object
category.p: Requests reservation of a name in one of the reservation namespaces supplied by the application's resource file.

offset-table.offset: Name to reserve.
Reservation class, such as AppName or ModName.

category.title: Filetype Object
category.p: Requests a filetype. This is the only variable-length object type. Its payload begins with a fixed part followed by three embedded file blocks.

offset-table.offset: Application name associated with the filetype.
Requested filetype name.
Boolean flag indicating whether double-click loading is supported.
Size in bytes of the sprite file data.
Sprite file contents. The user interface requires RISC OS filetype &FF9.
Size in bytes of the product description file.
Product description file contents. The user interface requires filetype &FFF.
Size in bytes of the file format description file.
File format description contents. The user interface requires filetype &FFF.

category.p: For this object only, the stored size field in the object header is the size of the serialised fixed part plus the three size words and the three variable-length payloads. It is not simply sizeof(FileType).

category.title: Error Block Object

offset-table.offset: Description of the module or purpose requiring the error numbers.

category.title: Podule Object
category.p: Requests a podule number, and optionally a manufacturer number.

offset-table.offset: Boolean flag. Non-zero means a manufacturer number is also required.
Existing manufacturer number when allocate_manf is zero; otherwise the field is not significant.
Podule name.
Description of the podule.
Description of the use for the tagged object range.
Description of the use for the Draw object range.

category.title: Filing System Object
category.p: Requests a filing system number and its selector name.

offset-table.offset: Filing system name, such as SCSIFS.
Selection name used to choose the filing system, such as SCSI.
Description of the module or interface using the service calls.

category.title: Device Object
category.p: Requests a device number for the Device Claim protocol.

offset-table.offset: Device name.
Description of the device or its use.

subsubsection.title: Version Handling
subsubsection.p: The current source defines a single request-file version, written as 100 for version 1.00. The loader accepts any file whose version field is less than or equal to 100 and rejects newer versions.

version-table.version: Canonical format defined by the supplied sources and written by current Allocate binaries.
No distinct earlier on-disk request-file layout is defined in the supplied source tree. Older files, if any exist, are accepted only if they are binary-compatible with version 1.00.
Rejected by the current loader as an unknown future format.

subsubsection.title: Developer Details File
subsubsection.p: Allocate also stores the requestor details separately. Current versions read from Choices:Allocate.Details first and fall back to Allocate:Details. When saving, current versions attempt to write <Choices$Write>.Allocate.Details and fall back to Allocate:Details.
The standalone details file is simply the 452-byte written on its own, with RISC OS filetype &FFD.

The bundled help records one behavioural change across application versions: older Allocate versions stored user details within the application directory, whereas later versions prefer the Choices location.

subsubsection.title: Email Export Format
subsubsection.p: The Save for email operation creates a plain text file of filetype &FFF. The file starts with a human-readable summary of every request object, then appends a uuencoded attachment named request containing the full binary request file.
Each summary block is generated from the in-memory object data. The binary attachment is produced by first writing a normal request file and then uuencoding that file in the traditional form:

subsubsection.title: Reserved-Names CSV Export
subsubsection.p: The Save reserved names operation writes only reservation objects. The output file has filetype &DFE and contains one comma-separated line per reservation:

definition-table.definition: Current date formatted as dd-Mon-yyyy in UTC.

subsubsection.p: The export routine does not quote commas within fields, so any commas present in the stored data will split columns in the resulting CSV.

subsubsection.title: Reply Form Export
subsubsection.p: The Save reply form operation writes a plain text template of filetype &FFF. Objects are grouped by their type identifier order, from SWI chunks through devices. Section headings and per-object labels are taken from the application's messages file.
The generated text is intended as a response template from the allocations service rather than as an interchange format. It contains no machine-readable version field.

subsection.title: Minimal Request File Layout
subsection.p: The following pseudocode shows the layout of a request file containing one reservation object:

p.extended-example: write 8 bytes "ALLOCATE" write int32 100 write int32 1 write DetailsInfo write int32 2 ; Object_Reservation write int32 60 ; sizeof(Reservation) write 40 bytes reservation text write 20 bytes reservation type

revision.author: Charles (Gerph)
revision.title: Initial version
revision.change: Documented the Allocate binary request file, embedded object layouts, developer details storage, and the related email, CSV, and reply-form exports from the supplied sources and bundled help.
